CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Dunraven and his friends are still nibbling. They
want a conference now. The Home Rule Bill does not
satisfy them, and we suppose they fear that it looks
like business and_ that it will pass in spite of them.
Dunraven and those others who signed the recent, pub-
lished statement throw over the Carsonites when they
say — * (7) We desire, moreover, to dissociate our-
selves from the fears expressed in Ulster and elsewhere,
that under any system of Irish Government' Protes-
tants would be exposed to religious and civil disabili-
ties. We unhesitatingly record our conyictions that
whatever results the settlement of this question has
in store for us, religious intolerance or civil oppression

need not be feared.””  Well, it is about time that some
of these men came out with such a statement. When
do the Protestant traders of Cork, Limerick, Water-
ford, Tralee, Kilkenny, and other places intend to make
a like statement ?

The sickening old paper run by the Arnott’s, thab
was recently insulting Lady Aberdeen, 1s all of a
wobble. The ‘“ grim >’ humbug in the North-East hav-
ing "been jeered off the stage, the ‘* Irish Times’’ has
got political influenza, and this precious call for a con-
ference by Dunraven and Co. has given it a bad fit of
the shivers. The ‘‘ Irish Times,”” the libeller of Canon
MecInerney and the insulter of Lady Aberdeen, is in-
deed o contemptible paper.

Is the *“ Irish Times’’ preparing to.rat on Carson?
If Carson next week should bring up a few of his
wooden cannon we suppose Arnott’s paper would get a
start and recommence on the ‘ grim’’ game: it is a
contemptible paper, There is nothing very grim about
the following, which we take from Arnott’s paper :—
We have never declared ourselves unreasoning
advocates of any fixed policy. We are Unionisty
because, while we have daily experience of the
advantages of the Union, we have never been pre-
sented with a scheme of Home Rule which, in our
opinion, was acceptable on its merits.

In other words “Alf” is in a funk and is wobbling.
The wretched paper goes on:— °
Our attitude towards the present riome Rule
Bill has been governed by these considerations.
We have not rejected this Bill merely because it
is a Home Rule Bill. We have subjected it to
critical examination as a scheme of Trish govern-
ment, and we have found it, on its merits as such,
a thoroughly bad scheme.

The Home Rule Bill has got to be passed, and the time
is gone by, we fear, for conferences.  Why did not
Dunraven and his friends come oub publicly against
the Belfast atrocities? We are not aware that he or
any of his fellow conferenceites sent subscriptions to
the fund for the relief of the victims of Belfast intoler-
ance. Now that Saint Carson has proved a damp
squib it was to be expected that Dunraven would try
to get a finger in the pie, and the ““Trish Times ™ 1s
the journalistic Vicar of Bray.

The following letter from a great granddaughter of
0’Connell was received in time for publication last
weelk, bub as we were dealing with the matter in our
notes we held it over :—‘Darrynane Abbey, Co. Kerry,
October 10. Dear Sir,—May I, through your columns,
draw the attention of your readers to an illustrated
article which has appeared in the of
October 1st (a copy of which I now send you), and
which has been quoted in various national papers in
this country. The ‘ gentle lady of English birth’ who
is appealing for funds in the — ——— has not
hesitated to stoop to do so by caricaturing the people
amongst whom she is living. In one picture we see
the interior of an Irish cottage in which the inmates
are represented as living in company with fowls and
pigs—we -actually have the ‘ pet pig’ clasped in the
arms of the farmer in the same manner in which ‘gentle
ladies’ are wont to hold their lap-dogs. In another
place we see the ‘ mud and stone cabins,’ presumably
of the village of ‘ Ballincoona.” T have lived all my

RN

life in south-west Kerry, -and until Miss Brodrick
* started her building and called it * Ballincoona,’ T had
never heard the name - applied to any village or town-_
land in this district. The ‘mud and stone cabins’ in
the photograph are ‘part of the “town of
Sneem, on Col. Wardin’s estate, 10 miles from Miss
Brodrick’s residence. I should like to correct a few
mistakes in the correspondent’s article.
He mentions that Miss Brodrick started an Irish Col-
loge in'Caherdaniel. The College (which Miss Brod-
rick attended as a pupil) was started by the Caherdaniel
Gaelic League with the surplus funds from the Caher-
daniel Feis of 1910. The College committee consisted
of the Rev. J. Burke, Rev. J. Lynch, Mrs. O’Shea,
N.T.; Padraig O'Shea (secretary), and Michael Keat-
ing. Most of the farmers in the parish of Caherdaniel
are now owners of their farms and have (through the
help of the Congested Districts Board) comfortable,
well-built. houses, and ecertainly object to being held
up to the -English public as ‘ Irish poor” living in ‘mud
and stone cabins.” As to the nursing work in the dis-
trict, for seven years there has been one of Lady Dua-
ley’s district- nurses working here. - She has not yet
found it impossible to attend the sick in_their own
homes. When I say she has successfully attended 85
midwifery cases since last January I think I have said
enough on the subject of nursing. T have already
written to a national paper on the subject of the Caher-
daniel Gaelic College and my letter has not been pub-
lished. I think as the great granddaughter of the
Liberator, born within the historic walls of Darrynane,
I am entitled to at least as much space in Irish news-
papers as Lord Midleton’s sister!—Yours truly,
Brmrexy O’CoNNELL ™

Miss Brodrick is certainly a lady of great energy and

force of character. The snapshot of the man fondling

a pig cannot be got over, and one explanation we re-

ceived places the blame on the snapper and exonerates

Miss Brodrick. We see by the papers that Miss Brod-

rick has just gone to America, so that we cannot have

her explanation, if she cares to offer one, for some

time. Tt appears that it .was not until Miss Brodrick

-started her work that a branch of the National Health

‘ Association was started by residents in this part of
Treland, and it is said that the branch established is a

mere association on paper. Miss Brodrick has learned

Trish, whilst we are told that the Lady Dudley nurse

does not know the language. The learning of Irish is

a point in anyone’s favour, and we are informed that

Miss Brodrick came to the help of the Irish Text So-

We have no recollection of having heard of Misson the Probationer Staff: Formerly these appoint-

Brodrick’s name until our attention was called to the
photographs in the English ha’penny picture paper,
and the picture for English popular consumption of
the man fondling the pig is calculated to arouse any
Inish person’s indignation, and if that picture has placed
Miss Brodrick in a false position she has only the Eng-
lish snapper to blame.

The. Catholic Defence Society has made inquiries
into ‘' tolerance’’ as practised in the Belfast Union.
1t will be remembered that Mr, Devlin in the House of
Commons on May 6th stated that of the total salaries
paid by the Guardians of the Belfast Union in 1911
(£16,700) the amount paid to Catholic officials came
to £680 only, of which £447 was paid to chaplains,
etc., whom it was compulsory by law on the Guardians
to appoint. There was a storm of indignation at the
next Board meeting in Belfast, and the chairman
stated that ‘‘ there was not a scintilla of truth in that
statement. TInstead of being only £230, it happened
that the salavies paid to these officials van into thou-
sands of pounds in the year.”” Emboldened by the
chairman’s assurance Mr. Fee, a Protestant Guardian,
gave notice that he would move for a return showing
the religion, salavies, etc., of all officers in the vi
of the Board of Guardians. Had the chairman’s s
ment been justified, here was an immediate oppor-
tunity to confound Mr. Devlin, and vindicate the Board
(composed of 54 Non-Catholics and 4 Catholic Guar-
dians), against the charge of intolerance levelled against
it. . But the ‘‘ tolerant *’ majority of the Belfast Guar-
dians evidently considered Mr. Fee’s curiosity incon-
venient, and refused the return by 24 votes to 4! Sub-
sequently a Catholic Guardian (Mr. Savage), endea-
voured to obtain the information by asking for a copy
of the annual return supplied to the Local Government
Board, but with no better success.

The statement of the Catholic Defence Society re-
marks :—*‘ The refusal of the Belfast Guardians to pro-
duce figures in support of their repudiation -of the
charge of intolerance virtually amounts to a plea of
‘ guilty,” but in order that no doubt on the matter

_ should remain in the public mind, the Council of the

Catholic Defence Society (a purely non-political body),
have gone carefully into the latest published Abstract
of Accounts of the Belfast Union with the following
result :—Eliminating from the total salavies the
amounts paid to chaplains; etc., of all denominations
whom it is compulsory on the Guardians to appoint,
and the remuneration of the Probationer Nursing Staff,
the method of whose appointment is dealt with Jater,
the salaries paid to Catholics (£507) amount to 8.1
per cent. of the nett total salaries (£16,340) in the gift
of the Gusvdians. Catholics form 24 per cent. of the
population of Belfast, so that on the basis of popula-
tion their share of the Union salaries should amount

_rightly to about £4,000 instead of £500. No salary

paid to a Catholic official appointed by the Belfast
Guardians reaches £100. Of the 16 Dispensary Medi-
cal Officers and 4 Apothecaries not one is a Catholic,
nor is there a single Catholic on' the Visiting or Resi-
dent Medical Staff, nor on the Clerical Staff.  Little
wonder the ¢ tolerant’ Belfast Guardians found Mr.

Fee’s motion ‘inopportune.

““ As regards Probationer Nurses, Belfast Union has
the advantage of possessing an excellent Training
School for Nurses, and many Catholic gitls, not from
Ulster alone, but from all parts of Ireland, are at-

* tracted by advertisements of examinations for positions

ments were made on the result of an Educational Test
Examination, all candidates passing the examination
being put on a  qualified:list,” and called for duty as
vacancies on the staff arose, no further examination
being held until the ‘qualified list* was exhausted.-
In this way Catholic girls got a perfectly fair chance of
appointment, as they were not dependent on a vote of
the Board, and a large proportion of the positions fell to
them. But a few years ago a change was made in the
method of appointment. A fixed number of vacancies,
about one half the average pass list was inserted-in the
advertisement of examination, and as the order of
merit in passing the examination is not taken into ac-
count, fthis system allows the Board to vote as to
which of the successful candidates shall be placed on
the ‘ qualified list.” So great is the spirit of toleration
in the Belfast Union that during the period this sys-
tem has been in operation, 110 Catholic girls, success-
ful in the examination, have been refused election, and
not one single Protestant!’’

Does farming pay? A County Wexford farmer,
Mr. J. Rudd, was fined 20s. and expenses for driving
a motor car in Dublin' at a speed dangerous to the
public. Mr. Rudd’s motor car collided with a pony
and cart. Mr. Rudd stated that he was the owner
of the motor car; and was, ‘‘on the evening’in ques-
tion,”” returning from the Pheenix Park Races. Does
farming pay?  Certainly one would' be inclined: tc
answer in the affirmative when he reads of a County
ford farmer being fined 20s. for running down a
y cart and pony, whilst he—the County Wexford
farmer—was retwning in his own motor from the
Pheenix Park Races.

So Queen Bess's English Institution in Ireland is
to remain outside the scope of this bold, bad Irish
Parliament—Trinity College is to be a bit of English
soil in Treland. The anti-Irish crowd whose attitude
seems to be that the Nationalists could not be safely
trusted with the custody of a Unionist sixpence is very
petty and mean, and it must now and again irritate
cven level-headed Irishmen. But after all it.may be
as well that Trinity sticks true to its traditions “and
clects to remain an English Institution in Ireland.

Now that Trinity has been °safeguarded,” those
weak persons who favour making County Council
Scholarships tenable ‘at that anti-Irish and bloated
institution may reconsider their position. The move
for the exclusion of Trinity and the Queen’s, of Bel-
fast, shows a very nasty anti-Irish spirit, but after all
it may be just as well that Trinityis painted up in its
true colours for all men to see and note.

Dr. Starkie and. his precious ‘‘ National '’ Board
have precipitated a situation in connection with the
summary dismissal. of Mr. Mansfield that probably
was the best thing that could have happened for the
National Teachers. In the end it appears to have
narrowed itself down to a demand that Mr. Mansfield
should *‘repudiate publicly ’’ something which he
believed to be true. Mr. Mansfield declined to do
this, and he. was dismissed. The matter that he was
to repudiate publicly concerned an unpopular
inspector named something like Whelp. It so happens
that Mr. Mansfield is.an exceptionally efficient teacher
and Vice-President of the Irish National Teachers’
Organization. -

The present inspection of schools is in many cases
atrocious. We have heard of a teacher who was

‘What sort of & man is this Dr. Starkie? He seldom
appears in public. ~We suppose he is the National
Board, for we doubt, except when some particularly

- anti-Irish thing is to be done, if even Tony Traill gives

much attention to the. business. We are reminded
that at Belfast last year Dr. Starkie made some
remarks in the course of a speech which go to indicate
that he is ““a man of sentiment.”” Here is a fine
sentimental platitude which no doubt the ancient
Greek scholar thought the height of eloquence. Said
he :—* Without manliness and self-respect a teacher’s
influence is nought with his pupils, and the Board is
determined to support any measure calculated to raise
the status of the teaching profession, which hitherto
has not received a tithe of the honour and respect due
to those to whose hands the rising generation are
committed for better or for worse.”” There is a fine
old Joseph Sourface veneer about that; and yet this
is the man whose Board calls upon Mr. Mansfield to
repudiate publicly what Mr. Mansfield believes to be
true, and who summarily dismissed him because he
refused to do anything of the sort.  And then Dr.
Starkie prates in public about the virtues of manliness
and self-respect! When the teachers in Circuit 18
have the manliness and self-respect to protest against
Inspector Whelp, or whatever his name is, it is
another story. Well, a crisis has been precipitated
now. The Montenegrins, a nation consisting of less
people than are in Dublin, have gone out to fight
Turkey; are the National Teachers expected to shiver
and cower before Starkie and Whelp?  The whole
country will wateh the fight between the Teachers on
the one side and Starkie and Whelp on the other with
engrossing interest. )

We promised last weel that we might have some-
thing to say this week of the Surveyors’ and
Engineers’ Departments of the G.P.0., Dublin.
Ireland is divided, postally, into three surveyors’ dis-
tricts, Northern, Midland, and Southern. BEach
district is in charge of a surveyor. These surveyors
control the postmasters in their respective districts and
have the final say in all matters before submitting
them either to the Secretary or to the P.M.G. It can
be seen, therefore, how much the postmasters and
their staffs are at the mercy of these officials. Need-
less to say, all the surveyors, being chief officials, are
““ Saved.” The following is the list:—

Northern District—G. A. Whiteman,
about £800 a year.

Southern District—D. W. Harkness, ‘‘ Saved,”
about £900 a year.

Midland District—W. Brown,
£675 a year. ;

Any Masonic or other ““ Saved ’* lamb looking for a
soft and fat job in Ireland can go to headquarters with
his petition, and judging by results, he receives a most
sympathetic hearing.

CORRESPONDENCE.
Tue CATHOLIC UNIVERSITY CHURCH, STEPHEN’S GREEN.

Sir,—Might I venture to call attention to a subject
which I think is well deserving of attention on the part
of those whose duty it is to make all possible provision for
the moral and : eligious welfare of the 800 Catholic students
of University College, Dublin, at least on the Sundays and
the solemn festivals of the year. Now there is one very
simple and practicable means of meeting this necessity :
and that is to have at least one of the Masses in Universily
Church on Sundays and Iloly days set apart exclusively
for the students and professors { the College, and to have
suilable sermons preached at this Mass for students and
professors, a very large proportion of whom would he
certain to attend this Mass and sermon.

I believe this church is the property of the Irish Catholic
Bishops, and surely both the Bishops and Governing Body
of the College can arrange to have a special Mass and
sermon on Sundays for the Catholic students, instead of
having them hearing Mass in scattered units throughout
the entire city in such churches as they may choose.
Surely the parents of these young men in different parts
of Ireland have a right to look for all necessary facilities
for the practice of religious duties by their sons, whom
they have sent to this University College.

Yours truly, =
CATHOLIC IRISHMAN.

gether in centres where large developments of
factory production take place. The wealth of Belfast
is rather problematical, as the facts concerning: the
assessment for Income Tax there compared with Dublin
suggests: but Belfast is a manufacturing city depend-
ing to a big extent on woman and child labour, and
the number of ‘‘ hands,”’ or human machines, that at
the expense of a few shillings a week each, it keeps:
working for a large number of hours is certainly enor-
mous.  Whether that fact indicates wealth in any
rational or national sense of the word may be doubted :
but it certainly stands for a big development of modern
ind\{strin]ism, One Belfast is quite enough for Ire-

B ELFAST SWEATED GIRL WORKERS.

NOT in the whole civilized world, is there, at the pre-
sentstime, such utter heartless slavery as the work of
the great spinning mills of Belfast. It would be an eye-
opener for some of the philanthropic members of the Irish
Tealth Association to just pass an hour in these over-
heated mills, where the young half-clad girls pass their
weary days of racking toil. The speed ol the machinery
Is tremendous and necessitates the closest attention ol the
spinner. Ends of flaxen fibre are continually breaking,
and if allowed to pass would result in a spoilt  hank,”
which would spell “ fines ** on pay-day. And to avoid this,
the young womanly body is doomed to a stooping position
for the entire work-day, and it is easy to see how this ends.
Just as healthful exercises develop the frame, the un-
natural posture weakens and contracts it. No inspector
or health officer pays heed to this wanton waste ol health,
and the months and years pass, bringing premature age
and infirmity to the Belfast mill-girls. One me dly
note in the dense armies of working women, which blacken
the highroads of the great <ity, the sallow haggard faces
and attenuated forms of the overwrought slaves. LEven
the barefooted half-timers have an old, worn appearance.
Little mites in their earliest teens seem to have decades
of toil imprinted on their ashy checks, and are all tod
surely qualilying for the fell consumption microbe. In
connection with the hall-timers, it is a s ma on the hig
Belfast mili-owners that they do not sup footgear to
their little slaves. A few years back a pitying English
actor, on tour in Belfast, was so struck with the misery
of the barefooted child population, that he started a fund
for supplying boots to the needy little ones, heading the
list himself with a generous subscription. Needless to
tell, it was not supported, and the humbugs who spill
money like water at Covenant time, cculd not afford a pair
of hoots for a cold and shivering child. Then take the
case of the wareroom stitcher, and what do we find ?
LEven after the exposure of the sweaters, who exact
hundreds of stitches for a penny, the game goes on, and
at the present moment stitchers are required to topsew
four corners of sheets, each corner four inches long, for
three half-pence a dozen. And pillow-case workers do
the same at three farthings a dozen. Finally, the lot of
" the Belfast shopgirl is as bad. In the cheap goods stores
the-hours of employment are iniquitous. Girls are hard
at work from 9.30 a.m. till 8 p.m. An hour’s interval is
given for dinner (outside) and twenty minutes for indoor
tea. Wednesday is half-holiday, but this spell of leisure
is compensated by extra time heing exacted on Friday
nights till ten o’clock ; and on.Saturdays till 11.30, and
oftener midnight, the obliging shopwalker keeping the
shop door open for the belated customers. The muni-
ficent salary attached to these posts is 8s. per week : and
as board and lodging runs to this sum, where is the clothing
found 2 | = 5 SFALLS.”

N Thursday, October 10th, a meeting of Carsonites

was held at the Theatre Royal, Limerick, to

protest against Home Rule, and indirectly and incidentally,
1 presume, against Papistry, for have we not heen told
* times out of number that Home Rule has ceased to be a
political (uestion and has become a purely religious
affair! I shall not attempt‘to describe the motley crowd
that foregathered to vent their political bile and religious
spleen, in this city of the Violated Treaty, nor shall I re-
count thescenes that occurred subsequently. Sufficient to
say, that Catholic and Nationalist® Limerick, which had
‘been reading of the daily wanton and brutal savagery
of Belfast bigots, and of the gross calumnies against its
citizens, lost control of itself owing to the wooden-headed

~ gonduct of the police authorities, who introduced mounted
- policemen. when it was absolutely unnecessary to do so.

for the peace ol the city, we should have very serious

way, how many Protestant clergymen used their pastoral

All this by way of preface. Now to the point.

From the Carsonite bigots in the theatre my mind flung
itsell back on the past to that month of October, 1691,
when tho Treaty of Limerick was signed, and the Irish
papist began to lift his head and hope. I took up Leniliin’s
* History of Limerick ”’ and read the first article of the
Capitulation : 1, “ That the Roman Catholicks of this
Kingdom shall enjoy such privileges in the se of their
religion as are consistent with the laws of Ireland, or as
they did enjoy in the reign of Charles the Second ; and
their majesties (as soon as their affairs will permit them
to summon a Parliament in their Kingdom) will endeavour
to secure the said Roman Catholicks such furthur security
in that particular, as may preserve them. {rom any dis-
turbances upon the account of their said religion.” A
page or two further on Lenihan concludes his story of the
siege in these words : *“ Such was the termination of King
James’s hold on Ireland. Such was the end of the eveni~
ful struggle which Limericl. made lo secure freedom for
Irish Catholics. The Treaty was signed ! The Capitula-
tion was made. The articles agreed to! Alas! that it .
should be told how soon the Treaty was broken !—how
speedily faith was violated !—how rapidly English perfidy
exhibited the cloven foot when Ireland was again in her
power ! On the 3rd of Octobher the Treaty was signed !
On the 22nd of the same month the English Parliament
excluded Catholics from the Irish Houses of Lords and
Commons, by compelling them to take the oath of supre-
macy before admission.” No comment of mine is neces-
sary on all this. - I then took up Lecky’s ¢ Ireland in
the Eighteenth Century,” and this is how he closes the
first chapter of the frst volume : ‘‘ The heroic defence ‘of
Londonderry had already turned the scale in favour of
William ; the disaster of the Boyne and the surrender ‘of
Limerick destroyed the last hopes of the Catholics. They
secured, as they vainly imagined, by the Treaty of Limerick
their religious liberty ; but the bulk of the Catholic army
passed into the service of France, and the great Confisca-
tions that followed the Revolution completed the ruin ‘of
the old race. When the 1Sth Century dawned, the ‘great
majority of the former leaders of the people were either
sunk in abject poverty or scattered as exiles over Europe:
The last spasm of resistance had ceased, and the long
period of unbroken Proleslant ascendancy had begun.’?
Thus Lecky, Unionist and Protestant.

Londonderry and the Boyne have been utilised with
the utmost skill to band together and rally the forces of
the Protestant Ascendancy. The virgin city and ‘the
Prentice boys are yearly commemorated, and their story
told and retold. The Boyne has been made the back-
ground of every ‘Orange demonstration. The pious,
glorious, and immortal memory -of William the Protestant
is celebrated in toast, in speech and in song. The children
in the schools and their parents in the churches, hayve the
Battle of the Boyne burnt into their memories. The
annual July celebration fills Orangemen the world ‘over
to the bursting point. Let us learn from them and profit
by the lesson. We Irish Catholics lack a historical
rallying ground. The Manchester Martyrs incident,
however it be regarded as the expression of a movement,
is unworthy as the historical rallying ground of the
nation. ‘Some very outstanding central fact is essential.

It was at Limerick that Irish Catholics made their last
stand for freedom. From the North, with its bitter
memories of Derry and the Boyne; from the West,— .
Galway, Sligo and ‘Athlone ; in a word, from all over the
country marched these desperate men to make a last

desperate stand for faith and fatherland.- They fought
lile heroes, and capitulated on the most honourable terms.
‘What followed is written in letters of blood in the Penal
‘Code. T need not enlarge on this, yet I cannot refrain
from queting the oft-quoted words of Edmund Burke and
Dr. Johnson. Edmund Burke describes the Penal System
as follows: It was a machine of wise and elaborate
contrivance, and as well-fitted for the oppression, im-
poverishment, and degradation of a people, and the debase-
ment in them of human nature itself, as ever proceeded
from the perverted ingenuity of man.” Dr. Johnson
says: “The Irish are in a most unnatural state, for we
there see the minority prevailing over the majority.
There is.no evidence, even in the Ten Persecutions, of
such severity as that which the Protestants of Ireland have
exercised against the Catholics.”

The Orangeman looks to the Boyne for inspiration ;
and the Papist, if he will, may turn to Limerick. Pro-
testant Ascendancy is rooted round the Boyne; Irish
valour, Catholic persecution, and British perfidy by the
walls of Limerick. The pendulum is swinging gradually
round. Time is a wonderful adjuster. The land has
passed from the spoilers to the despoiled. Other changes
are at hand. God forbid that we Irish Catholics should,
in the day of victory, ever return evil for evil. But, is
it just to ourselves, or to our past, that we should forget
the sufferings of our fathers, or fail to commemorate
them ? 'We have nothing to be ashamed of, but rather
everything to glory in, from the consideration of those cruel
times.

The 12th of July every year brings back to the Northern
Protestants and the Orange exiles in Canada or elsewhere,
the memory of the Boyne, and fuses them together as one
in heart and one in purpose. Why should not each
recurring 3rd of October (or some day in the immediate
proximity) gather together in spirit round Limerick’s
storied walls the scattered children of the Gael: not in
tears and despondent as the Jews by the wailing wall in
Jerusalem, but joyful, buoyant, self-reliant, proud of
their fathers who braved all and lost all in the struggle
for faith and fatherland? On that day let us tell and
re-tell and tell once again the story of the struggle of our
country for religious and national freedom. Tell our
children in the school, until their little hearts beat fast,
and tdeir pulses throb, and their souls are fired at the
hearing : Tell thair parents in' the church at what uery
great price the Faith has been bought—and the price of
the blood of our nation’s martyrs: tell the world in the
public square that we are not unmindful of. those who
suffered and died that we might be free mon in a free
land. Let October be for us what July is for the Orange-
man, but without the savagery and the grossness. ‘ Re-
venge, remember Limerick ” was the cry of the Irish
Brigade as it dashed to victory at Fontenoy. ‘ Remem-
ber Limerick ”’ be our rallying cry too; but—we shall
wipe out “ Revenge ”: both the word and what lies behind
it. We forgive from our hearts, but we cannot forget. It
would be unreasonable and hopeless to expect so. The
pain has gone, the wounds are nearly healed, but the
scarg remain for ever,whether we will so or not.

1t devolves on Limerick in an obviously special manner
to take the lead in this, and thereby put another claim
on the gratitude' of the Irish nation, SARSFIELD.

HILE Belfast deserves all the evil advertisement it
is getting, it would, I think, be a mistake to
allow the impression to get abroad that the city is over-
‘whelmingly .cut-throat. It is not even overwhelmingly
against Home Rule. By bread alone doth the Belfast
man live, and he often does questionable things to come by
the bread. Changing his name, going smash, selting
fire to his premises (insured), are amongst the’ things
he has done for his bread in the past, and now he doesn’t
mind signing Carson’s Covenant for it. For the Covenant
is nothing more or less than a “ Test.” You sign the
Test if you wish not to be hoycotted. Carson gets credit
for inventing the document, but, as a matter of fact, the
old ladies of the Unionist alliance kept pestering our wives
and servant-girls to sign the thing all last winter. There
is nothing material to lose and there is trade to gain, or,
at any rate, retain by signing. As for being followers
of Carson on principle, they are not quite such fools.
Covenant Day itself was a farce even in Belfast. The
crowd in the city was a mere Saturday crowd compared
with a big meeting in Dublin, and, as for enthusiasm,
everybody I saw seemed to be looking at his neighbour
to see was the latter smiling. And the neighbour, in
many cases was smiling, for thousands were there, just
as I myself was, purely for fun. Even the decorations
told a tale of apathy. The Union Jack is such a stripey
concoction, a few of them in a breeze and the eye is dazzled
into imagining the whole place is Union Jacks. That
was the case on the 28th ; Belfast seemed to be all flags,
but when you stooped to count the flags you discovered
that not more than a third either of the people or the houses
were decorated. - In fact, you had but to contrast the
whole show with what you remembered of the Falls on the
occasion of any of Joe Devlin’s victories to feel that the
Orangeman is giving his last kick. He knows the game
is up.
busted. g .

with would be safeguards for the Catholics.of Belfast now

L am in agreement, and I heartily commend his sug-
gestions to all concerned. My contribution is not in-
tended in any spirit of carping criticism of his article,
but is rather in the way of amplification. There are
few Irishmen who have not visited Lisdoonvarna at
sonue time or another in quest of health or relaxation,
and all would like to know all about it and its sur-
roundings. It ought to be made as up-to-date as Har-
rogate, and as Irish as Harrowgate is English. 1t is
with the ardent desire of seeing this accomplished some
time or other that I offer this contribution, with some
suggestions as to what might be done, the fruit of 25
_years' acquaintanceship with the world.

Let me say outright that Lisdoonvarma is a very
much neglected health resort. When I say neglected,
I don’t mean any reference to a paucity of summer and
autumn visitors, but to the failure of the local people
to provide.adequate comforts and attractions for
visitors.

I knew the place 80 years ago intimately, and have,
from the various places of my exile, visited it frequently
in the interval, the last time as late as last summer,
and with the exception of two pump rooms and the pro-
vision of a few seats, and a kiosk for shelter, I could
discover-no improvement worth mentioning, effected in
this long interval. I except the result of the enter-
prise of a public spirited lady, who, within the past
few years has erected a fine hotel and laid out
a golf course. She is, I believe, the only hotel-owner
who caters for winter visitors. To one used to health
resorts in England and on the Continent, the whole
place looks ragged, unkempt, and dreary, even when
the weather is smiling, as it often does there. ~The
grass-grown, patchy lawns, and the almost complete
absence of flower-beds (I shall refer later on to trees),
are disagreeably noticeable. A town-planning scheme
would be a’ great improvement.  Parties should be
stopped from building lodges and cottages according
to their own haphazard ideas, which are, I fear,
primitive enough.  The thinkering with thatched
cottages should be abandoned. Everyone of them
I have seen deserves to be pulled down. Certainly,
nothing looks so nice and harmonious as a well-
thatched, neat cottage, but one, half-thatched and half-
slated, is an abomination and an eyesore. Of course
one will be .told that the people must® cut their coats
according to their means. This is false economy. A
few hundred borrowed pounds, invested in modernising
and enlarging, or rebuilding those lodges, would
come back five-fold in a few years. Other small
matters would also bear attention. I was never in the
two or three largest hotels in Lisdoonvarna, and so
cannot speak of their internal arrangements, which, T
am sure are free from exception, but in the smaller
hotels and lodges, useful bedroom furniture is much
needed, and the walls of all bedrooms might with

_advantage be papered or distempered. White-washed

walls are no doubt wholesome and sanitary, but these

_advantages are negatived by the lime getting into and

injuring one’s clothes. i :

whole county of Clare west of a line drawn diagonally’
from Killadysert on the Shannon, to Ballyvaughan on
Galway Bay, is bare and dreary and cold-looking: in
the extreme, all due to this lack of woodlands.  You

The lovely little glens between which Lisdoonvarna
‘stands are especially suitable for plantations.

They say that the strongest impression that one re-
ceives on first visiting Whitechapel, London, is of
noses. - They are prominent everywhere, owing to the
number of Hebrews about. Well, the outstanding im-
pression one gets in Lisdoonvarna, is of vast expanses
of land. A visitor once said to a jarvey, ' There is a
lot of land around here, and Lord ! how cold it looks."”
The jarvey, after glancing at him to see whether he
was merely a budding humorist, or the makings of a
fool, came to the conclusion that he was not of the
latter category, and replied, ‘“ Yes, they lave it out at
night.”” It was the same jarvey who once drove me
on a fine cloudy day, and when T asked him if he
thought it would come on to rain, said, ** "Iwill if it
keeps like this.”” I told him he would yet be put in a
book for that saying:

The number of places of historical and antiquarian
intevest within a radius of ten miles of Lisdoonvarna
is enormous.

In the Burren region there ave quite a dozen leaba

remains -of Firbolgian domination—and many small
ruins of churches of the sixth century and on-

_.wards. The Bwren glens are many, and run
-from east to west between high limestone ridges
with serrated tops. These glens are beautiful,
sunny, and warm, beyond expression, composed of the
richest of land, with low scrubby growths of hazel,

names that oceur to me are & Sleinn Crones¢, Fledd

It hreathes of intense love of all animals and small

. Lour correspondent was not far out in his ethnolo-
gical survey of the O’Loghlens, but I may remark that
about the dawn of Clare history all the district now
coxnpr1§ed in Corconiroe and Burren was occupied by
the “O’Conors of Corcomroe.  About the thirteenth
century, according to Frost, the clan divided in two,
one hrtm_ch taking the name of O’Loghlen, from an
ancestor— Mdotpeactam—and the O’Conors preserv-
ing their ribal name. The O'Loghlens took Burren
and nmintained a more or less independent existence
until 1701. The O’Conors, owing to internecine feuds,
got extinguished as an independent clan in the six-
teenth century, and their tribal lands passed to the
O’Brien’s, the lords paramount and big boys of the
whole county.

The O’Conor’s of Corcomroe were a warlike race,
their castles, in ruins, sentinel the coast, like Martello
towers, from Milltown-Malbay to Black Head, and
they haye left their name in Liscanor (Liop ui Concabaipe
and Lahinch (leatt ui Con¢idap).  Bounding the
O’Conor and O’Loghlen territories to the south and east
were the lands of the O’Deas, O’Quins, O’Griffys,
O’Hynes, O’Hogans, and MacClancys, fine sinewy
Dalcassian names, whose representatives are still very
numerous on the tribal lands.

So much for history. It is pleasant to know that
the parish priest of Lisdoonvarna is keeping up the
fine tradition of his predecessor, the venerable Father
Power, V.G, in ability to preach a good sermon in Irish.
Father Power was undoubtedly one of the finest priests
of his time, standing about .6 feet 4 inches in height
and with brains and good sense in proportion. He
died only a few years ago well up towards his hundredth
year. He was originally from Waterford, and how he
came to the diocese of Galway I don’t know, probably
as a young curate in search of health, which he must
have found, and so liked the place that he asked and
was allowed fo remain in the diocese. He was as far
back as I can remember the Vicar General, and, T am
told, just missed becoming bishop twice. It was as
an Irish preacher I knew him best. I have often
heard him preach and always in Irish. He
was a_terror to evildoers, but a rare hand at lifting a
lame dog over a stile. In the season he never preached
at Lisdoonvarna, preferring to say Mass at one or other
of the two outside parishes of Tuat Slae and Tuat
1hastena, where he could talk Irish to his own people.
Peace to his ashes.

I fear ‘‘ Visitor ”’ has been misinformed as to the
cause of the difficulty in getting land for the Hall in
Lisdoonvarna, and he blames the avarice of the land-
lords, of whom there are three, I believe, having each
a pull at the land there. Of course nothing is now too
bad to say of an TIrish landlord, everything is blamed
on him. Let us give the poor chap justice sometimes,
and rid ourselves of the fixed idea that all Irish tenants
are suffering saints.

That other was a Claddwh man. He was older and

loved it each in a different manner. The first man knew
Ingland and Europe, and his patriotism was the patrio-
tism of such a man. Ie loved the whole of Ireland and
the Claddagh as a part of Ireland. But the second man
had never left the Claddagh save once, long ago, when he
went to Dublin on an excursion for one day. His first
love was for thoe Claddagh, and he loved Ireland because
it was, so to speak, the boundary of the Claddagh.

Now these men were talking, and the tall one had asked
by way of introduction if that was indeed the Claddagh
of which he had heard and read. The older man answered
—with a smile that betrayed his pride—indeed it was.

““ And is this the statue of the Claddagh King? » said
